text: The surgeon initiates the gastrectomy by entering and dissecting through the gastrocolic omentum, extending the dissection to the patient's right. The gastrocolic omentum is further opened, and dissection proceeds along the short gastric vessels to separate the stomach from the spleen.

During the dissection, the surgeon identifies and includes any malignant tissue, such as metastatic nodal disease, within the omentum as part of the specimen. The stomach is freed from the ventral surface of the pancreas.

The surgeon continues the dissection along the edge of the colon, moving cephalad until reaching the duodenum. The dissection proceeds through the short gastric vessels, with careful attention to maintain proximity to the spleen.

The surgeon uses bipolar cautery to carry the dissection along the undersurface of the liver, mobilizing the duodenum. The gastropathic omentum is opened, and the dissection proceeds to the right crus and along the ventral surface of the esophagus.

The surgeon opens the gastrohepatic omentum widely in a stellate fashion to facilitate dissection across the gastroplog vessels. The duodenum and pylorus are identified, ensuring no antrum is retained on the duodenal stump.
The surgeon divides the duodenum and folds the duodenal stump over the ventral surface of the pancreas to minimize the risk of blowout. The more proximal portion of the stomach is then divided.

The surgeon transects the left gastric artery and its nodal arcade, incorporating them into the specimen. The dissection progresses toward the right crus and the gastroesophageal junction, leaving a tubular segment of the stomach along the greater curvature.

The surgeon staples the stomach and divides the remaining tissues using bipolar cautery. The excised specimen is placed into an extraction bag and removed through the assistance port in the patient's left lower quadrant. The stomach is then brought into proximity with the jejunum using a barbed suture, creating a running suture line.

The surgeon completes the back row of the anastomosis, leaving sutures in place with needles engaged. The jejunum is then opened using monopolar scissors, preparing for the second row of the ventral portion of the anastomosis.
The surgeon uses a barbed monofilament suture to create the inner layer of the anastomosis, placing sutures in the middle of the back row and running them to each corner.

The surgeon begins suturing in the middle of the back row, sewing to one corner, then places the suture in the middle again, sewing to the opposite corner and around it. The ventral portion of the inner layer of the anastomosis is completed, and the suture is tied. The surgeon returns to the suture used for the back wall, runs it to the other corner, and ties it, completing the anastomosis.

The surgeon performs an endoscopy on the patient, applying liberal saline over the stomach to check for air leaks, with no bubbling observed. The surgical wounds are closed using Maxon sutures, followed by Vicryl sutures, and Steristrips for skin closure.
